alex-antic-sa.summary: Antic was one of several Coalition senators who crossed party lines on 1 September 2025 to support a One Nation motion establishing a Senate inquiry into the effects of high immigration on the economy, housing affordability, wages, infrastructure and social cohesion.

sources.title: Australian Senators Defy Party Lines to Back One Nation Immigration Inquiry

alex-hawke-mitchell.summary: Hawke, a former Liberal Immigration Minister, has called for the government to resolve the status of roughly 7,500 long-term asylum seekers, saying: 'I think the Government should resolve this now. People have been here a long time and the reality of their condition is that they will be staying here.'

sources.title: RCOA welcomes Liberal ex-Minister's call to end years of waiting

ali-france-dickson.summary: In November 2016, before entering Parliament, Ali France publicly urged then-PM Malcolm Turnbull to reconsider a proposed lifetime ban on asylum seekers who arrived by boat, writing an open letter and citing her surgeon, an Iraqi refugee who came to Australia by boat; she said genuine refugees 'should be given a chance.' Wikipedia characterises her as a critic of offshore detention. Her July 2025 first speech did not address immigration.

sources.title: This Woman Owes Her Quality Of Life To A Refugee Who Came Here By Boat
Amputee saved by refugee surgeon urges PM to rethink asylum seeker ban
Ali France

alicia-payne-canberra.summary: Payne criticises Australia's immigration detention as 'destructive and expensive' and argues indefinite detention is unjustifiable. She supports abolishing temporary protection visas, expanding the government-funded humanitarian intake (citing Labor's 27,000-place commitment) plus a 5,000-place community-sponsored program, increasing UNHCR funding, and accepting New Zealand's offer to resettle refugees.

sources.title: Refugees - 23/06/2021

alison-penfold-lyne.summary: In her first speech, Penfold said: 'We must return to an immigration policy founded on "Team Australia" not "hotel Australia", one where our shared values matter—the basis of our multicultural success story,' advocating a values-based, selective approach to immigration.

sources.title: Alison Penfold first speech to Parliament

allegra-spender-wentworth.summary: Spender's platform includes support for skilled migration and humanitarian treatment of refugees; she is a member of the Joint Standing Committee on Migration and authored an opinion piece (Australian Financial Review, June 2024) arguing for a rational, economically grounded approach to migration.

sources.title: Allegra Spender

andrew-bragg-nsw.summary: In a December 2025 address to the Centre for Independent Studies on housing, Bragg said 'cutting migration will not singularly fix the housing crisis, but reducing it will help demand,' and argued migration policy should prioritise bringing in people 'who can actually build homes,' noting Australia is short 80,000 tradies while receiving only 4,000 on visas in the prior year.

sources.title: Address to the Centre for Independent Studies: A Liberal Approach to Housing

andrew-gee-calare.summary: Gee, an independent MP, championed the case of the Bathurst-based Nguyen family to remain in Australia, welcoming the Immigration Minister's grant of visas after a petition of over 22,000 signatures, saying it 'brings to an end a decade of stress and uncertainty for this much-loved Bathurst family' and was 'proof that people power can prevail.'

sources.title: Victory for People Power! The Nguyen Family Can Stay! Minister Approves Visas!

andrew-giles-scullin.summary: As Immigration Minister, Giles issued Ministerial Direction 99 (2023), which tightened deportation criteria by weighting a non-citizen's community ties and time in Australia. After criticism, on 7 June 2024 he replaced it with Ministerial Direction 110, which he said makes 'community protection' and 'the safety of the Australian community' the highest priority in visa cancellation decisions; he stated he had cancelled 40 visas 'in the national interest' that week. As Shadow Minister he led Labor's campaign against privatising Australia's visa system and established a Multicultural Caucus Committee.

sources.title: Andrew Giles
Direction 99: Andrew Giles announces government will replace controversial immigration directive

andrew-hastie-canning.summary: Andrew Hastie has stated that Australia's immigration numbers are 'too high, while the standards are too low,' arguing population growth has outpaced infrastructure and pressured housing and services and that 'the system needs a massive overhaul' (March 2026). In October 2025 he resigned from the Liberal shadow frontbench, saying he could not remain Shadow Home Affairs Minister 'and remain silent on immigration policy.'

sources.title: Andrew Hastie rallies the right on immigration levels at conservative conference in Perth
Andrew Hastie quits Liberal front bench over immigration row
Andrew Hastie

andrew-leigh-fenner.summary: Leigh, a Labor MP and economist, publicly defends immigration, stating in 2026 that the government has 'reduced migration from the record highs it was under the Coalition.' He has more broadly framed Australia's non-discriminatory and skilled migration system as a source of national strength.

sources.title: Andrew Leigh can't stop lying on immigration

andrew-wilkie-clark.summary: Wilkie considers Australia's bipartisan policy of mandatory detention 'immoral and illegal under numerous international agreements.' His Ending Indefinite and Arbitrary Immigration Detention Bill 2022 would effectively abolish offshore detention and move asylum seekers and refugees into the community with access to housing, support and the right to work. He cites detention costs of about $360,000 per person per year versus about $4,429 for someone living in the community, and condemns Australia's offshore processing on Nauru and Manus.

sources.title: Immoral and unlawful treatment of asylum seekers must end

andrew-willcox-dawson.summary: Willcox backs the Coalition's values-based migration plan, saying "Since Labor took office, the migration program has lacked direction: numbers have soared while standards have plummeted" and that "Living in Australia is a privilege, not a right: those who come here must respect our laws, share our values, and contribute to our nation." He supports reducing immigration numbers, raising standards, and making the Australian Values Statement binding for all visa holders.

sources.title: Coalition to Restore Integrity and Common Sense to Migration

angus-taylor-hume.summary: On becoming Liberal Party leader in February 2026, Taylor named immigration his "top priority" and called for lower immigration and a policy that "puts the interests of Australians first," stating Australia should "deny access of immigrants from high risk regions who bring violence and hate."

sources.title: Angus Taylor

anika-wells-lilley.summary: In response to the nationwide anti-immigration 'March for Australia' rallies of 31 August 2025, Wells condemned them as 'awful scenes' involving 'known racists,' saying 'we all have a responsibility to speak up and assure people from migrant communities ... that we stand with them and this isn't Australia.'

sources.title: Interview, Sky News Newsday
Thousands rally against immigration with neo-Nazis taking the spotlight

anne-aly-cowan.summary: As Minister for Multicultural Affairs, Aly stated in June 2025 that 'multiculturalism isn't solely about social cohesion and multicultural communities aren't solely responsible for social cohesion,' arguing that making such communities 'solely and wholly responsible for social cohesion' had added to a perception of them being 'over-securitised.' In 2016 she spoke against then-Immigration Minister Peter Dutton's comments regarding Lebanese Muslim immigration.

sources.title: Multicultural communities are not entirely responsible for social cohesion, says minister
Anne Aly

anne-webster-mallee.summary: Webster has stated 'Albanese Labor has brought in 1.4 million people in just 3 1/2 years,' arguing excess immigration has added pressure to housing, infrastructure and services and that Labor has 'flooded' the economy with population growth driven by immigration. She endorses the Coalition's tougher immigration stance, citing measures such as a binding Australian Values Statement visa condition, restoring Temporary Protection Visas, a Safe Country List, and enhanced visa screening.

sources.title: Dr Anne Webster MP - The Nationals take tougher stance on immigration through Coalition policy

anthony-albanese-grayndler.summary: As Prime Minister, Albanese's government in February 2023 abolished temporary protection visas, allowing up to 19,000 asylum seekers to stay in Australia permanently. In his 1996 maiden speech he expressed support for multiculturalism.

sources.title: Anthony Albanese

ash-ambihaipahar-barton.summary: In her first speech, Ambihaipahar, of Tamil Sri Lankan and Papua New Guinean heritage, embraced multicultural Australia, stating 'While Barton championed the White Australia policy, I live and breathe multiculturalism' and describing herself as 'the walking, talking embodiment of modern Australia,' committing to fight racism and discrimination.

sources.title: First Speech - Ash Ambihaipahar MP - Member for Barton NSW

barnaby-joyce-new-england.summary: In September 2015 Joyce called for Australia to accept more Syrian refugees, with a preference to prioritise Christian refugees. In 2016 he opposed One Nation's proposed Muslim immigration ban, and he has stated his opposition to banning the burqa.

sources.title: Barnaby Joyce

bob-katter-kennedy.summary: Bob Katter (KAP) called in February 2024 for Australia's migrant intake to be 'cut in half' and the student intake reduced by two-thirds, saying migration preference 'has to come from countries with democracy, egalitarian traditions, rule of law, Christian principles and industrial awards.' On asylum seekers he advocates turning back boats 'with, of course, fuel and food.' In January 2026 he reiterated a policy of 'No migration without assimilation.'

sources.title: Katter calls for immigration cuts after Ipswich fatal stabbing
Migration Legislation
Katter calls for 'No Migration without Assimilation'

bridget-mckenzie-vic.summary: In her 2025 CPAC speech, McKenzie said she is "pro-migrant and against aggressive immigration," warning against an immigration program that strains housing, infrastructure, services and social cohesion. She said Australia should welcome migrants who share its values and respect the rule of law, stated "We do not need Islamists in this country," and noted Australia has avoided the illegal-immigration problems seen elsewhere partly due to tough decisions by previous Coalition governments.

sources.title: CPAC Speech 2025 - Brisbane

cassandra-fernando-holt.summary: Fernando, who came to Australia from Sri Lanka in 1999 and sits on the House Standing Committee for Migration, said in a July 2023 interview that "Labor is the party that has ... promoted multiculturalism and ensured a 'fair go' for all Australians" and described Australia as "a diverse, inclusive and accepting country." On Sri Lankan boat arrivals she said the Australian Government "has full faith in their Sri Lankan counterparts when working on challenges of mutual interest, such as people smuggling."

sources.title: Interview with Cassandra Fernando MP, prominent Australian politician of Sri Lankan origin

chris-bowen-mcmahon.summary: As Minister for Immigration and Citizenship (2010-2013), Bowen secured an offshore processing arrangement with Malaysia (the "Malaysia Solution") in 2011, under which Australia would transfer asylum seekers who arrived by boat to Malaysia in exchange for resettling refugees from there; the arrangement was struck down by the High Court in September 2011.

sources.title: Australia's asylum seeker policy history: a story of blunders and shame

clare-o-neil-hotham.summary: As Minister for Home Affairs, in a National Press Club address on 27 April 2023 O'Neil described Australia's migration system as 'broken' after 'a decade of genuinely breathtaking neglect,' saying it 'is failing our businesses, it is failing migrants themselves. And most importantly, it is failing Australians.' She commissioned a review of the migration system and indicated her proposed reforms would likely produce 'a slightly smaller migration program over time.'

sources.title: National Press Club: Australia's Migration System with Q and A
Clare O'Neil

dai-le-fowler.summary: Le, an independent and former refugee, takes a conditional view of migration, saying that if migration places are increased the government 'has the duty to ensure that there are immediate plans to build more housing, more public transport infrastructure and more local services,' and that government should 'first look locally.' She notes her electorate resettled nearly 10,000 refugees without additional infrastructure funding, and pledges to celebrate cultural diversity.

sources.title: Dai Le MP's First Speech in Parliament

dan-tehan-wannon.summary: As Shadow Minister for Immigration and Citizenship, Tehan has criticised Labor over migration levels, stating 'this Labor Government is pursuing Rudd's agenda by stealth' and 'Labor is delivering a big Australia without being upfront with the Australian people.' He has argued the government lacks a migration plan addressing impacts on rents, infrastructure and congestion, and raised concern about the whereabouts of tens of thousands of unsuccessful asylum-seeker applicants.

sources.title: Shadow Minister Dan Tehan slams Labor's immigration policy

daniel-mulino-fraser.summary: In his maiden speech, Mulino, who was born in Italy and migrated to Australia with his family in 1970, praised Australia's multicultural tradition, saying 'Australia is not unique in having a large foreign-born population, but I don't believe that any other country is better at knitting together disparate communities.' He highlighted the diversity of his electorate of Fraser and framed migrants as central contributors to the community.

sources.title: Dr Daniel Mulino (ALP-Fraser) - Maiden Speech

dave-sharma-nsw.summary: In his March 2024 Senate first speech, Sharma linked high immigration to housing pressure, saying 'the shortage of housing is being exacerbated by high immigration' and that 'until we are able to accelerate the pace of our home building, we need to reduce our immigration intake.'

sources.title: Sen. Dave Sharma (NSW-Lib) First Speech

david-batt-hinkler.summary: Batt promotes the Coalition's Australian Values Migration Plan, stating "Since Labor came to power, Australia's migration program has been in chaos. Numbers have been too high, and standards have been too low," and "To protect our way of life and restore Australians' standard of living, we must reduce immigration numbers and lift migration standards." He supports a values-based migration system that prioritises the Australian Values Statement in assessing permanent visa applications.

sources.title: Migration plan first instalment

david-pocock-act.summary: Pocock opposes offshore processing, calling for an end to it and a more human-rights-compliant refugee approach. After the government reauthorised Nauru offshore detention in February 2023 he called it a 'massive failure' and argued it 'makes no sense to be paying $420m of taxpayer money over three years to detain a small group of people.'

sources.title: David Pocock blasts Albanese government as it reauthorises Nauru offshore immigration detention
Immigration & Refugees

david-shoebridge-nsw.summary: As the Greens spokesperson on immigration, Shoebridge opposes the Labor government's resettlement deal with Nauru, calling for it to be 'ripped up' and for 'an immediate pause on sending any further people to Nauru,' and has called for a royal commission into offshore processing.

sources.title: Calls for 'immediate pause' of 'secret' Nauru deal

dean-smith-wa.summary: In July 2018, Smith called for a national debate on Australia's population as it reached 25 million, saying issues were broader than immigration alone and included regional growth, congestion and infrastructure. He advocated "moderation" of intake and better forecasting so Australia could "prepare and plan better," suggesting an audit-commission approach or parliamentary inquiry to lead the debate.

sources.title: Government senator Dean Smith urges national debate about population

deborah-o-neill-nsw.summary: O'Neill says the best way to retain the cohesiveness of Australia's multicultural society is to support an increased humanitarian intake and ensure adequate funding for resettlement of refugees and migrants, while protecting borders from 'chaos.' She has said Australians 'really rejected the sense of chaos, of processes being overwhelmed.'

sources.title: Politics a way of giving action to faith
sources.publisher: The Sisters of the Good Samaritan

ed-husic-chifley.summary: In his maiden speech, Husic, the first Muslim member of federal parliament, praised migrants and multiculturalism, stating 'multiculturalism represents a vast reservoir of energy this nation can tap into,' and that harnessing talent 'from the first owners to the recently arrived' builds 'one of the greatest countries on the planet.'

sources.title: Ed Husic (ALP-Chifley) - Maiden Speech

elizabeth-watson-brown-ryan.summary: No personal position was found in fetched sources. Per the Australian Greens platform (the party she represents): seeking asylum is treated as a human right, with commitments to end offshore processing, oppose indefinite/arbitrary detention, increase the humanitarian intake, and run a migration program prioritising family reunion and humanitarian programs.

sources.title: Immigration and Refugees | Australian Greens

garth-hamilton-groom.summary: Hamilton publicly rebuked One Nation's Pauline Hanson over her call for a "monocultural" Australia, saying her "appeal to a monoculture had more in common with sharia law or North Korea than it did with western liberal democracies." He defended Australian multiculturalism against One Nation's calls to end multiculturalism and cut migration.

sources.title: One Nation craves mainstream appeal, but Pauline Hanson's bleak vision of Australia shows she's firmly on the fringes
sources.publisher: Bytes Europe (republishing The Guardian, by Tom McIlroy)

ged-kearney-cooper.summary: In her first speech (May 2018), Kearney advocated for a 'humane refugee policy' and called for an end to offshore detention of asylum seekers, describing indefinite offshore immigration detention as 'shameful.' She has supported medevac legislation allowing detained refugees to access medical care.

sources.title: Ged Kearney

helen-haines-indi.summary: Haines criticised the Coalition's immigration policy (April 2026) as 'disingenuous at best, divisive at worst,' arguing humanitarian obligations to refugees and asylum seekers are 'separate to skilled and temporary migration' and that existing laws already require migrants to commit to Australian values and pass language tests. She celebrated Australia's diversity and opposed 'singling people out based on where they come from, including people fleeing the war in Gaza.'

sources.title: Haines slams Coalition immigration policy as 'disingenuous at best, divisive at worst'

jacinta-nampijinpa-price-nt.summary: On 3 September 2025 Price claimed the Labor government was giving preference to immigrants from particular countries, specifically India, citing concern over 'large numbers' arriving. She later retracted the claim that immigration law permits ethnic preference but said 'I don't believe I have anything to apologise about'; on 10 September 2025 Liberal leader Sussan Ley removed her from the shadow ministry after she refused to apologise.

sources.title: Jacinta Nampijinpa Price
Jacinta Nampijinpa Price's comments on migration continue to cause headaches for the Coalition

jacqui-lambie-tas.summary: Lambie's stated immigration positions have shifted over time. In 2014-2017 she proposed banning the burqa, introduced a bill to prohibit full face coverings during terrorism threat declarations, called for Australia to adopt travel restrictions on Muslim-majority countries similar to Donald Trump's, and advocated deporting Muslims who supported Sharia law. After participating in the 2018 program 'Go Back to Where You Came From,' she shifted toward a more pro-refugee stance, saying the discussion about accepting more refugees "needs to be on the political table," and distanced herself from earlier anti-Islam rhetoric.

sources.title: Jacqui Lambie

james-paterson-vic.summary: Paterson defended the Coalition's plan to reduce migration, arguing it would bring in 140,000 permanent residents in a way that is "much more sustainable than what Labor has been doing" and would "ease pressure on the housing market, health sector and congestion."

sources.title: Dutton doubles down on migration

jamie-chaffey-parkes.summary: In his first speech, Chaffey (Nationals) highlighted 'the need for targeted immigration' as a longstanding regional priority and emphasised attracting 'skilled and talented workers, and professionals' to grow the population of the Parkes electorate.

sources.title: First Speech – Jamie Chaffey MP

jason-clare-blaxland.summary: As Education Minister, Clare announced a cap of 270,000 international student enrolments for 2025 to create 'a better and a fairer system' by returning enrolments toward pre-pandemic levels, framing it as a response to housing affordability concerns linked to record migration and a crackdown on 'shonks' exploiting the sector.

sources.title: Australia caps international student numbers amid discontent over housing

jason-wood-la-trobe.summary: Wood chaired the Joint Standing Committee on Migration from 2013 and served as Assistant Minister for Customs, Community Safety and Multicultural Affairs (2019-2022). During 2016-2019 he campaigned prominently on law-and-order issues relating to so-called 'African gangs', later saying he had 'no regrets' and rejecting accusations of racism. From 2022 he held shadow responsibility for community safety, migrant services and multicultural affairs.

sources.title: Jason Wood (politician)

jenny-mcallister-nsw.summary: Responding in the Senate to anti-immigration marches, McAllister said the government has 'zero tolerance for hate and discrimination in all its forms' and described anti-Indian sentiment seen at the protests as racist, emphasising a commitment to 'building a safer and more inclusive Australia for everyone.'

sources.title: Faruqi accuses Senate of 'normalising' and 'legitimising' hate after anti-immigration marches

jerome-laxale-bennelong.summary: Laxale emphasises support for multiculturalism in one of Australia's most multicultural electorates, stating 'I am incredibly proud to represent an electorate which is among the most multicultural in this country' and listing 'enhancing multiculturalism' among his priorities.

sources.title: About – Jerome Laxale

jessica-collins-nsw.summary: In her first Senate speech (30 July 2025), Collins drew on her doctoral research with the Karen refugee community resettled from Burma to Brisbane and time spent in refugee camps on the Thai-Burma border, stating that 'as a middle power, Australians have a moral obligation to help where we can, understanding the limitations we have.' She called for expanded economic sanctions on Burma's military regime.

sources.title: Collins, Senator Jessica: 30 Jul 2025: Senate debates

jim-chalmers-rankin.summary: As Treasurer, Chalmers defended the government's migration management, stating migration was 'already down 45 per cent since its peak,' and the government emphasises housing supply over migration cuts; per Labor platform, Labor caps permanent migration at 185,000 and targets net overseas migration of around 225,000.

sources.title: How One Nation, Labor, the Coalition, and the Greens compare on migration policy

jordon-steele-john-wa.summary: In his maiden Senate speech, Steele-John called for a more humanitarian approach to refugees and asylum seekers, describing the situation on Manus Island as an "ongoing humanitarian crisis" and arguing Australia is "a nation built by those who come across the sea seeking a better life."

sources.title: Sen. Jordon Steele-John (Greens-WA) - Maiden Speech

josh-burns-macnamara.summary: Burns has advocated for refugees and asylum seekers, moving a parliamentary motion urging the government to free refugees held in onshore and offshore detention (referenced June 2021). His advocacy is shaped by his family's history as migrants who fled persecution in Europe.

sources.title: Josh Burns (politician)

josh-wilson-fremantle.summary: In his September 2016 first speech, Josh Wilson said 'indefinite detention is wrong' and that 'the operation of the centres at Nauru and Manus Island has been unacceptable,' arguing that 'creating a properly constituted regional settlement framework is possible.'

sources.title: Josh Wilson (ALP-Fremantle) First Speech
First Speech: Mr Josh Wilson MP

julian-hill-bruce.summary: Hill has championed Australian multiculturalism and 'the importance of Australia embracing its human diversity.' He made 'no apology' for championing reforms to the regulation of international education that were likely to reduce the number of international students granted visas, drawing criticism from the international education industry.

sources.title: Julian Hill (politician)

kara-cook-bonner.summary: Cook condemned the anti-immigration "March for Australia" rallies, saying they "were not patriotic gatherings; they were parades of division, fear and prejudice" and that "those who preach division tear at the social fabric of our nation." She stated Australia "was at its best when it was inclusive, diverse and welcoming."

sources.title: Kara Cook Condemns March for Australia Rallies

kate-chaney-curtin.summary: Speaking on 26 March 2024 as the Migration Amendment (Removal and Other Measures) Bill 2024 was forced through the House, Kate Chaney said it was 'very very disappointing,' stating her community 'wants to see compassion when we are approaching asylum seekers.' She has also criticised the former 'fast track' processing system as flawed.

sources.title: Forcing the Migration Act Through The House - 26 March 2024
Speeches | Kate Chaney MP

kate-thwaites-jagajaga.summary: In her maiden speech (July 2019), Thwaites supported a 'humane approach to asylum seekers,' adding 'I believe this can be achieved while maintaining our sovereignty and our borders.' She has criticised the holding of asylum seekers in hotels as 'unsafe and cruel conditions,' said 'unnecessary cruelty is a hallmark of this government when it comes to treatment of asylum seekers,' and criticised refusal to take up New Zealand's resettlement offers.

sources.title: Kate Thwaites (ALP-Jagajaga) - Maiden Speech
Asylum Seekers

katy-gallagher-act.summary: At a 2015 Palm Sunday refugee rally Gallagher expressed discomfort with offshore detention, telling Fairfax Media 'I am uncomfortable with what is going on now' and that few Australians, 'if they fully understood what was going on in detention centres and offshore processing, would be that comfortable with how it is being conducted.'

sources.title: ACT leads moves to soften ALP refugee policy

larissa-waters-qld.summary: In a public statement on her official Facebook page (31 May 2024), Waters said 'we should be proud of multicultural and migrant communities, but the Labor and Liberal parties want to demonise migrants to distract from their own failings.' As Greens leader she has rejected cuts to overall migration numbers and the framing of migrants/international students as a cause of the housing crisis.

sources.title: Larissa Waters Facebook post on multicultural and migrant communities

leon-rebello-mcpherson.summary: In his first speech, Rebello argued Australia "face[s] a population surge fuelled by a reckless immigration profile, one that no longer reflects the careful planning or national interest," and that "the desire to come to Australia, while admirable, should not be the sole qualification. It is the willingness to contribute to Australia that must be the true test." He warned poorly managed migration risks "rising social tensions, buckling infrastructure, divided communities," while describing a multicultural society as "a strength, not a strain" when migration is well managed.

sources.title: Maiden Speech - Leon Rebello LNP

lidia-thorpe-vic.summary: Thorpe is a vocal opponent of immigration and offshore detention. Attending protests at the Melbourne Immigration Transit Accommodation in 2022 over the transfer of detainees to Christmas Island, she demanded to know "how many people seeking asylum are actually in there, and what are the plans for them?" and criticised the treatment of detainees. She has continued to advocate for asylum seekers and refugees in the Senate.

sources.title: 'No regard for human life': Lidia Thorpe demands police explain violent clashes with protesters outside Melbourne immigration centre

malcolm-roberts-qld.summary: Roberts advocates cutting Australia's annual skilled and family migration intake to around 70,000 (a reduction of more than 60% from the 190,000 cap), describing this as 'zero net' migration, and has called for a parliamentary inquiry into the impacts of mass immigration on housing, wages, infrastructure and social cohesion.

sources.title: One Nation's Malcolm Roberts wants migration rate more than halved
Malcolm Roberts (politician)

maria-kovacic-nsw.summary: In her first speech, Kovacic (daughter of Croatian migrants who fled communism) described Australia welcoming her parents and 'hundreds of thousands like them' as an act of a country with 'extraordinary courage and confidence,' framing her migrant story as part of Australia's multicultural history alongside its Indigenous First Peoples.

sources.title: First Speech

marion-scrymgour-lingiari.summary: Speaking as Labor candidate for Lingiari, Scrymgour said she wants 'to be able to put the compassion back into politics, especially when we talk about important issues like Indigenous affairs and immigration.'

sources.title: 'Put compassion back into politics': Marion Scrymgour — Labor candidate for Lingiari, NT

mark-dreyfus-isaacs.summary: As Attorney-General, Dreyfus promoted the Albanese Government's reforms to the refugee protection system (October 2023), stating Australians are 'rightly proud of our country's generous refugee program' and that reforms would give 'a fair go to genuine asylum seekers' while breaking 'the business model of people who seek to exploit the system' and not allowing 'people traffickers and other bad actors to exploit vulnerable migrants.'

sources.title: Restoring Integrity to our Protection System

matthew-canavan-qld.summary: On a speech page about migration/protection legislation, Canavan supports strict border control and deterrence of unauthorised boat arrivals, taking pride that the government 'put a stop to' people smuggling, and supports requiring asylum seekers to take reasonable steps to establish their identity and make protection visa applications in good faith. He argues these measures are consistent with international obligations and cites the reduction of children in detention.

sources.title: Migration Further Protection Legislation

mehreen-faruqi-nsw.summary: As the Australian Greens' immigration and citizenship spokesperson, Faruqi advocates for refugees and asylum seekers and seeks an end to mandatory detention. In September 2025 she moved in the Senate to condemn anti-immigration marches as 'racist, white supremacist rallies' and accused politicians and media of normalising hate.

sources.title: Faruqi accuses Senate of 'normalising' and 'legitimising' hate after anti-immigration marches

michael-mccormack-riverina.summary: In a 2018 interview on regional migration, McCormack said "all options are on the table" for improving migration policy and supported "encouraging strategic targeted migration" to fill regional skill shortages, stating "Migrants bring a willingness to work. They start small businesses and their contribution is highly valued" and that regional migration "needs to be at the forefront of our immigration system."

sources.title: Riverina MP Michael McCormack: "all options are on the table" in response to claimed five-year regional migration visas

milton-dick-oxley.summary: In his first speech to Parliament (12 October 2016), Dick described Labor as "proudly the party of multiculturalism" and said "multiculturalism is not just a great social strength of our nation but increasingly a great economic strength." Referencing migrants and refugees, he said they "proudly call Australia home and are entitled to feel welcome—not targeted or demonised" and pledged he "will never judge anyone by their race or religion."

sources.title: First Speech to Parliament, 12 October 2016

monique-ryan-kooyong.summary: Ryan states that 'having clearly-defined, realistic, sustainable, and transparent immigration policies doesn't have to erode our humanity,' and says she has 'consistently voted against the Albanese Government's punitive laws criminalising asylum seekers and punishing those seeking refuge in this country,' describing such laws as contravening human rights obligations.

sources.title: What a second Albanese Government means for refugees

murray-watt-qld.summary: In relation to the August 2025 memorandum of understanding allowing Australia to send members of the NZYQ cohort to Nauru, Watt said the arrangement would involve 'hundreds' from that cohort, the group of detainees whose visas were cancelled but who could not be deported.

sources.title: By sending non-visa holders to Nauru, Australia is shifting its responsibilities

nick-mckim-tas.summary: As Greens immigration spokesperson, McKim is a strong opponent of offshore detention, describing it as designed "to make people's lives so miserable... that they decide to turn back to face the persecution they fled." He has called Australia a "human rights pariah," said there is "never an excuse for torture," criticised Labor's policy as "the same rubbish in a different bin," and called for detainees to be evacuated and a "genuine regional approach" to be adopted.

sources.title: Australia is "A Human Rights Pariah": Senator Nick McKim on Evacuating Offshore Detainees to Safety

patrick-gorman-perth.summary: In his first speech to Parliament, Patrick Gorman said 'Perth welcomes new Australians and has a proud migrant history,' highlighting his electorate's multicultural and migrant heritage.

sources.title: First Speech — Patrick Gorman MP

paul-scarr-qld.summary: In an address to the Migration Institute of Australia, Scarr (Shadow Minister for Immigration) argues net overseas migration is 'materially higher than a range of measures of long-term sustainable levels' and points to research suggesting around 160,000-220,000 annually as optimal for GDP per capita. He frames concern about immigration numbers as driven by housing and economic pressures rather than opposition to multiculturalism, calls for evidence-based debate that does not 'inflame emotion,' and celebrates Australia's refugee resettlement legacy.

sources.title: Address to the Migration Institute of Australia

pauline-hanson-qld.summary: Hanson advocates drastically reduced immigration, having called for capping immigration (75,000 in 2018; 130,000 in 2025). In a June 2026 speech she declared Australia "cannot be a multicultural society" and must become monocultural. She has called for a ban on Muslim immigration and has stated Australia is "in danger of being swamped" by groups she says do not assimilate. Per reporting on a September 2025 Senate immigration inquiry she backed, her broader advocacy includes deeper migration cuts, an eight-year wait for citizenship and welfare, and withdrawal from the UN Refugee Convention.

sources.title: Pauline Hanson
Australian Senators Defy Party Lines to Back One Nation Immigration Inquiry

penny-wong-sa.summary: As Foreign Minister, Wong said her government would "consider New Zealand's concerns about Australia's Section 501 deportation policy," under which non-citizens with criminal records are deported, an issue that had strained relations between the two countries.

sources.title: Penny Wong

peter-khalil-wills.summary: Khalil advocates a welcoming posture toward immigrants, crediting his parents' 1970 migration from Egypt, and stated migrants commit to Australia because of 'our values of freedom, of equality of opportunity, of a fair go.' During the COVID-19 pandemic (2020) he called for releasing detained refugees and asylum seekers who had cleared security checks, arguing it would protect their health and help slow the spread of the virus.

sources.title: Peter Khalil

phillip-thompson-herbert.summary: Thompson supports the Coalition's plan to reduce migration, saying "Right now, the system is out of control. Numbers are too high and standards are too low," and "To protect our way of life and restore living standards, we must reduce migration and raise the bar." He argues that Australians should not be competing with foreigners for housing and that "Australians must come first."

sources.title: Media Release: Australian Values Migration plan

rebekha-sharkie-mayo.summary: On her 'Where I Stand' page Sharkie states that 'Our immigration intake should be matched to our capacity to appropriately accommodate new migrants and balanced with social and economic benefits,' arguing immigration policy should be considered holistically alongside capacity in healthcare, housing and transport, with regular reviews of intake numbers.

sources.title: Where I Stand

richard-marles-corio.summary: Marles supports turning back asylum-seeker boats and a Pacific Solution-style offshore resettlement of refugees, and as Shadow Minister for Immigration and Border Protection (2013-2016) backed maintaining offshore processing to deter people smuggling. He also supported the Australian War Memorial commemorating Operation Sovereign Borders navy personnel.

sources.title: Richard Marles

sally-sitou-reid.summary: In her 2022 first speech, Sitou, the daughter of refugees who fled Indochina, celebrated Australia's transformation "from an embrace of a White Australia policy to a country that is now a majority-migrant nation," describing the White Australia policy as based on "fear and a failure of imagination." She praised former PM Malcolm Fraser's 1977 refugee policy as "on the right side of history" and said diverse representatives "make our parliament better and our democracy stronger."

sources.title: Sally Sitou: 'Good governments change lives. I know because they changed mine', First speech - 2022

sam-birrell-nicholls.summary: Birrell has advocated for regional skilled migration, supporting a Designated Area Migration Agreement (DAMA) for the Goulburn Valley and, in 2021, pushing before a parliamentary inquiry for 'an immediate global recruitment campaign to attract migrants with in-demand skills' to address regional job shortages.

sources.title: Sam Birrell

sam-lim-tangney.summary: In his maiden speech (6 September 2022), Malaysian-born Sam Lim credited former Labor PM Gough Whitlam with ending the White Australia policy and introducing multiculturalism, saying 'because of him we can call Australia home.'

sources.title: Malaysian-born MP Sam Lim makes trilingual maiden speech
Australia's second Malaysian-born federal MP discusses the road ahead

sarah-hanson-young-sa.summary: A former refugee-justice and Amnesty International campaigner and former Greens immigration spokesperson, Hanson-Young has been a prominent advocate against offshore detention. On leaving the immigration portfolio she said she would "never stop fighting for people seeking asylum" and that her team's work "has exposed the systematic abuse of women and children on Nauru and has been central to changing public perception about offshore detention."

sources.title: Di Natale takes Hanson-Young out of asylum seeker area
Sarah Hanson-Young

sarah-henderson-vic.summary: Henderson opposed the medevac legislation allowing medical transfers of asylum seekers to Australia, saying "there is no need for this bill ... this is all about politics" and that medical treatment was already available on Manus Island and Nauru. She has generally supported the party line on boat turnbacks and offshore processing.

sources.title: Sarah Henderson

sean-bell-nsw.summary: Bell named 'ending mass immigration' among his top Senate priorities, arguing that high immigration levels are driving housing unaffordability, overloading infrastructure and pushing up energy prices in NSW, and calling for a government that puts 'Australian families, industries, and values first.'

sources.title: Incoming One Nation senator vows to end mass migration

simon-kennedy-cook.summary: In his 2024 first speech, Kennedy criticised high migration levels, saying "record migration is being used to paper over the weakness of the Australian economy" and that population was growing faster than at any time since 1952, outpacing housing supply. He called to "match migration levels with the ability of the building industry to supply new homes," while arguing "the one area where we do need to dramatically increase migration is migrants with skills in construction related industries."

sources.title: First Speech — Simon Kennedy, Federal Member for Cook

steph-hodgins-may-vic.summary: In her first Senate speech, Hodgins-May criticised "this country's abhorrent approach to refugees and asylum seekers seeking our protection," cited a record 120 million people forcibly displaced worldwide, and called for "more humility and compassion in our treatment of refugees and asylum seekers" rather than "cruel campaign slogans designed only to scare people and win votes."

sources.title: First Speech - Senator Steph Hodgins-May

steve-georganas-adelaide.summary: In his maiden speech, Georganas, the son of Greek migrants, spoke positively about immigration's contribution to Australia, stating 'There is no doubt that Australia's migrants have made this country a better place' and that the hopes and dreams of migrants 'have given this country an optimism, resilience, diversity and vitality that must be valued and nurtured.' He noted his electorate's many nationalities and languages.

sources.title: Steve Georganas (ALP-Hindmarsh) - Maiden Speech

susan-templeman-macquarie.summary: In February 2019, Templeman supported the 'medevac' bill enabling medical transfers of refugees from offshore processing facilities, saying the amendments "are about making sure sick and vulnerable people in regional processing facilities can receive the medical care they need, without compromising existing border protection arrangements," and accusing the Coalition of "misrepresenting ALP policy on this issue."

sources.title: Fight over asylum seeker bill comes to Macquarie

sussan-ley-farrer.summary: As Opposition Leader, Ley stated that "strong borders keep us safe, they also allow us to be generous and compassionate to those fleeing conflict."

sources.title: Politicians have scapegoated immigration for decades. It's time to flip the script

tanya-plibersek-sydney.summary: In a 2003 workshop talk on multiculturalism, Plibersek argued that "the first and biggest lie is the one that suggests asylum seekers are a threat to this nation," noting Australia accepts a small number of refugees by world standards. She criticised Australia for choosing "the most expensive system for dealing with refugees - locking them up - or even more expensive, putting them on Nauru or Manus Island," and is a vocal advocate for multiculturalism.

sources.title: Tanya Plibersek, ALP Member for Sydney: About Multiculturalism in Australia

tim-watts-gellibrand.summary: In his first speech (December 2013), Watts argued immigration and multiculturalism are 'foundation stones' of Australia's open society and that 'an open economy cannot reach its full potential without an equally open society.' While saying asylum seekers should arrive 'in a fair and orderly way,' he called the Coalition's 'dramatic cuts to Australia's annual refugee intake and aid budget' 'an abject moral failure,' and criticised renaming the immigration department to add 'Border Protection.'

sources.title: Tim Watts (ALP-Gellibrand) - First Speech

tony-burke-watson.summary: As Minister for Home Affairs and Immigration, Burke has defended immigration and multiculturalism, saying "Without immigration, we would be a very different country" and "Modern Australia and multicultural Australia are the same thing," and that "what matters is who you are, not where you're from." He has also said "there is no place in our country for people who seek to divide and undermine our social cohesion," and rejected the Coalition's proposed values-based screening of migrants.

sources.title: 'Would be a different country': Burke defends migration as Taylor policy criticism mounts
Politicians have scapegoated immigration for decades. It's time to flip the script

tony-pasin-barker.summary: In his 2013 first speech, Pasin, the son of Italian immigrants, said "I honour the rich contribution that immigrants have made to my home state of South Australia and the nation," and added he was "proud to be part of a government that is working to ensure that once again those who come to our great nation ... do so in a safe and orderly manner, respectful of proper processes."

sources.title: Tony Pasin (Lib - Barker) - First Speech

zaneta-mascarenhas-swan.summary: In her first speech, Mascarenhas recounted that her Goan Indian father was told at the Australian embassy in Kenya 'You have the right skills, but you're the wrong colour' under the White Australia policy, and said her family's migration was only possible after the Whitlam government dismantled the last parts of that policy; she praised 'the most multicultural parliament we've had in history.'

sources.title: Indian-origin MP Zaneta Mascarenhas delivers inaugural speech
Goan origin MP Zaneta Mascarenhas delivers her first speech
